food_name: Canh Chua Ca Loc (Vietnamese Sour Snakehead Fish Soup)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese sour fish soup (canh chua). The closest defensible FoodOn anchor is 'fish soup food product' (FOODON_03311737). Exact match unavailable.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Fish soup) is the closest defensible category. No specific code exists for Vietnamese sour fish soup in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Vietnamese dish. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular codes for regional ethnic soups.

main_ingredients: fresh herbs (rice paddy herb, Vietnamese coriander)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of canh chua cá lóc is approximately 500 g, representing a typical single-serve portion as commonly served in Vietnamese households and restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fish, snakehead, cooked, dry heat (NDB 15240) and component vegetables; composite estimate for Vietnamese sour fish soup
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for canh chua cá lóc exists in USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB Foodo, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for cooked snakehead fish (NDB 15240) combined with typical vegetable and broth components (tamarind, pineapple, tomato, okra, bean sprouts). The input record values (energy 56 kcal, protein 6 g, fat 2 g, carbohydrate 3.6 g per 100 g) are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained. Sodium (240 mg/100 g) reflects fish sauce contribution and is plausible for this dish. All values are flagged as estimated. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water are set to -1 as detailed breakdown data are not available from approved sources for this composite dish.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate based on tamarind-vegetable fish soup with low carbohydrate density
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for canh chua cá lóc in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 45 (low) based on the analogous category of mixed vegetable fish soups with tamarind and pineapple, which have low carbohydrate density (3.6 g/100 g) and moderate fibre. The tamarind and pineapple contribute fructose and organic acids that moderate glycemic response. Glycemic load per 500 g serving = (45 × 18 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 8.1 (low GL). This is a defensible analogous estimate; direct GI testing of this dish is recommended for higher confidence.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 240 mg per 100 g; a 500 g bowl delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium, representing 52% of the WHO daily limit of 2300 mg, primarily from fish sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce when preparing canh chua cá lóc at home, and limit portion size to 250–300 g to keep sodium intake below 600–720 mg per serving; the dish's potassium from vegetables and pineapple partially offsets sodium load but does not eliminate the concern.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (3.6 g/100 g), low GI (~45), moderate protein (6 g/100 g) from snakehead fish, and soluble fibre from okra supporting post-meal glucose moderation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Canh chua cá lóc is suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes due to its low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index; however, pineapple and tamarind contribute natural sugars, so portion control is advised for those with poorly controlled blood glucose, and no additional sugar should be added during preparation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.4 g/100 g) and lean fish protein are cardioprotective, but sodium content (240 mg/100 g) is a concern for cardiovascular risk management
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The lean snakehead fish provides cardioprotective protein with very low saturated fat, making the dish broadly suitable for cardiovascular health; however, the high sodium from fish sauce should be minimised by using reduced-sodium fish sauce or reducing the quantity used, particularly for individuals with established cardiovascular disease or heart failure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lean fish protein with some omega-3 fatty acids; anti-inflammatory herbs and spices; very low saturated fat; nightshade vegetables (tomato, chilli) present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Canh chua cá lóc is broadly suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its lean fish protein and anti-inflammatory ingredient profile, but individuals who experience symptom flares after consuming nightshade vegetables (tomato, chilli) should monitor their personal response and consider reducing or omitting these ingredients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Canh chua cá lóc is a classic Vietnamese sour fish soup originating from the Mekong Delta region of Southern Vietnam. The dish features snakehead fish (cá lóc, Channa striata) as its primary protein, simmered in a tangy, aromatic broth made with tamarind as the souring agent, fresh pineapple chunks, ripe tomatoes, okra, bean sprouts, and fresh herbs including rice paddy herb (ngò ôm) and Vietnamese coriander (rau răm). The broth is seasoned with fish sauce, garlic, and chilli, producing a complex flavour profile that is simultaneously sour, sweet, savoury, and mildly spicy. The texture of the soup is light and brothy with tender fish pieces and crisp-tender vegetables. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 56 kcal, 6 g protein, 2 g fat (0.4 g saturated), and 3.6 g carbohydrate with 0.8 g dietary fibre and 240 mg sodium. The low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index (~45) make it suitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes. The sodium level from fish sauce requires attention for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. It is commonly served as a shared soup course in Vietnamese family meals, particularly in Southern Vietnamese cuisine, and is widely available in Vietnamese restaurants across Southeast Asia including Singapore.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for canh chua cá lóc exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked snakehead fish and typical vegetables, and are consistent with the input record values. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (cooked snakehead fish NDB 15240, tamarind, pineapple, tomato, okra, bean sprouts) as no exact approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. Input record values are consistent with this estimate and have been retained. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid breakdown data for this composite Vietnamese sour fish soup are not available in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source as a single composite entry as of 2024. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/trace for this minimally processed fish and vegetable soup with no hydrogenated fats. 4. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on mixed vegetable fish soups with low carbohydrate density; direct GI testing of this dish is recommended. 5. Glycemic load per 500 g serving calculated as (45 × 18 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 8.1. 6. The input record listed country_of_origin as Singapore; this has been corrected to Vietnam (Southern Vietnam / Mekong Delta) as the dish's authentic origin, while noting it is widely consumed in Singapore. 7. Sodium of 240 mg/100 g is plausible for a fish-sauce-seasoned soup and is retained from the input record.
